office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, voting on state law, the state budget, and taxation for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton).
race_blurb: An open seat after Sen. Rebecca Saldaña declined to seek reelection. Sitting State Rep. Chipalo Street, backed by Saldaña and much of the Democratic establishment, faces first-time candidate and environmental-justice organizer Tatiana Brown; both prefer the Democratic Party, overlap broadly on policy, and advance to the general.

candidates.summary: State Representative for the 37th District since 2023 and Microsoft technologist, running for the open Senate seat with the outgoing incumbent's endorsement and a record of bills passed and district funding secured.
highlights: Says he secured $131 million in state investment for the district, including Garfield Super Block, Skyway homeownership, and a reparations study [S1]
Championed the 'millionaires tax' and cites passage of rent stabilization, tenant protections, and zoning reform to increase home building [S1][S2]
Cites work restoring abortion funding, maintaining food assistance for immigrants, banning ICE mask-wearing, and stopping Flock camera surveillance [S1]

taxes.basis: Championed the millionaires tax to fund education and healthcare access; wants the wealthy to pay more.

spending.basis: Record emphasizes securing state investment ($131 million for district projects) as the lever for outcomes.

housing.basis: Passed tenant protections including rent stabilization, while also reforming zoning to increase home building — tilts to protections with a supply component.

experience.basis: Sitting legislator since 2023 with Democratic leadership role, passed bills, and establishment backing — the record candidate in this race, though tenure is short.

immigration.basis: Cites work maintaining food assistance for immigrants, banning ICE mask-wearing, and stopping Flock camera surveillance.

social.basis: Cites legislative work restoring abortion funding.

tech.basis: Cites stopping Flock camera surveillance — a surveillance-limiting posture — while working in the tech industry.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate and community organizer; co-chair of the Washington Environmental Justice Council, running a small-dollar campaign that rejects corporate and PAC money and emphasizes participatory governance.
highlights: Co-chairs the Washington Environmental Justice Council implementing the HEAL Act, and helped shape advocacy around the Climate Commitment Act [S1][S2]
Rejects corporate donations, PAC, fossil fuel, and defense contractor money; wants government-funded community assemblies in policymaking [S1][S2]
Supports progressive revenue so 'the wealthiest pay their fair share,' plus education funding, public health, and alternative public safety approaches [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Platform repeatedly calls for progressive revenue so 'the wealthiest pay their fair share.'

spending.basis: Priorities center on funding K-12, higher education, public health, and transit as levers for outcomes.

safety.basis: Lists 'alternative public safety approaches' among her public-service priorities.

climate.basis: Climate-justice platform grounded in a record: co-chairs the Environmental Justice Council implementing the HEAL Act and helped shape CCA advocacy.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with an organizer profile and no elected experience, contrasting with a sitting legislator.

reform.basis: Explicitly refuses corporate, PAC, fossil fuel, and defense contractor money; small-dollar funded and emphasizes participatory community assemblies.
